entry.title: Re: Concern about "Inscriptions"
entry.summary:
In the recent discussions surrounding the mempoolfullrbf, it has been noted that only a small percentage of nodes (approximately 10%) are required to relay a particular class of transactions for these transactions to consistently reach miners. This highlights a significant challenge in altering network-wide transaction relay policies to prevent specific types of transactions, such as inscriptions, from reaching miners and being included in blocks. Despite efforts to update relay policies across the network, inscription users have demonstrated their ability to bypass these limitations by sending non-standard transactions directly to miners. An example of such a transaction can be viewed on [mempool.space](https://mempool.space/tx/0301e0480b374b32851a9462db29dc19fe830a7f7d7a88b81612b9d42099c0ae).
Furthermore, the dynamic nature of the scripts used in transaction witnesses complicates the enforcement of anti-inscription policies. Inscription proponents can alter scripts to circumvent new rules, necessitating continuous updates and widespread adoption of these changes by the network—a process that is slow and cumbersome, involving over 90% of network participants. In contrast, pro-inscription updates require acceptance from a much smaller, more motivated group of users and businesses, giving them an agility advantage in this technological tug-of-war.
The ongoing struggle between pro and anti-inscription factions suggests that technical solutions alone may not be sufficient to control unwanted inscriptions effectively. Economic measures, such as increasing the cost of block space to exceed the affordability or willingness of inscribers, may offer a more viable solution. This could involve leveraging Bitcoin's increasing utility and adoption to raise transaction costs or relying on the diminishing market demand for inscriptions as they transition from novelty to commonplace. These economic strategies depend on broader adoption and shifts in user valuation, pointing to a natural progression rather than immediate technical enforceability.
